context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i'm kinda lost on the spider diagram tbh, like idk what they even want me to put for the 5 pathways.
exchanges.tutor: That's a common place to feel stuck — but let's work through it together! Before we dig in, what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material? Even a rough idea of one or two of them would be a great starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling lost, which is a normal starting point. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways directly, I should use guided discovery to help them recall or locate what they already know from the course. I'll ask them what they already know about the 5 pathways to assess their starting point and encourage them to engage with the course material first.

exchanges.student: ngl i vaguely remember stuff like buildings/transport maybe? but it all blurs together rn and idk what counts for the diagram.
exchanges.tutor: You're not too far off! The course material actually defines all 5 pathways pretty clearly. Before going further, it's worth going back to the relevant course section to refresh your memory — trying to fill in the diagram from a vague recollection will make this harder than it needs to be. Once you've reviewed them, which city did you pick for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has some partial recall, which is a good sign. I should affirm that they're on the right track without confirming or completing the list for them. I'll encourage them to go back to the course material to ground themselves before attempting the diagram, since the pathways are defined in the course content. This is a case where redirecting to the source material is the right pedagogical move.

exchanges.student: i picked jakarta, but tbh even after rereading i still can't tell if they want policies, problems, or like random examples under each one.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice of city! Now, the assignment actually mentions there's an example spider diagram included — have you looked at that closely? What do you see in the example entries? That should give you a strong hint about what kind of content they're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified their city and is now asking a substantive question about what content goes in the diagram. Rather than explaining it for them, I should guide them to look more carefully at the assignment description and example provided. The assignment mentions an example spider diagram, which should clarify the format. I'll push them toward that resource and ask a question to help them reason through it themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah but the example kinda made me more confused lol, it felt super broad and i can't tell how detailed mine is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: Honestly, the best way to resolve that confusion is to just try one. Pick whichever of the 5 pathways feels most obvious for Jakarta, and tell me what you'd put under it. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting stuck on the level of detail required, which is a common anxiety. Rather than resolving this for them, I should redirect them toward productive action — trying something rather than overthinking the format. I'll ask them to attempt one pathway for Jakarta and see what comes up naturally. This is a case for guided discovery through doing.

exchanges.student: maybe transport? like flooding affects roads but also emissions? idk if that's adaptation or mitigation or if i'm mixing categories fr.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something interesting there. So — in your own words, what's the difference between adaptation and mitigation? That distinction might help you figure out where your Jakarta transport examples land.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a substantive and actually quite good observation connecting transport to both flooding (adaptation) and emissions (mitigation). Rather than confirming or correcting directly, I should ask them to think through the distinction between adaptation and mitigation themselves — this is a core course concept they should be able to apply. This moves the conversation in the direction of the solution.

exchanges.student: i always mix them up tbh, can u just explain the whole spider diagram setup in a simple way bc rn it's all mashed together in my head.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but working through it yourself is exactly how it'll click — I'm not going to lay it all out for you. Let's just focus on this one thing first: if Jakarta builds a seawall to protect against rising seas, is that changing the *cause* of climate change or *responding* to its effects? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything for them, which would violate the core Socratic and guided discovery principles. However, they are also expressing genuine confusion about a foundational concept (adaptation vs. mitigation) that is blocking their progress. I should not give them the full explanation, but I can ask a targeted question to help them reconstruct the distinction themselves. I'll keep it focused and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: responding i think, but then i still don't get how that fits with the 5 pathways thing bc some pathways sound like sectors and some sound like actions.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on adaptation — good. Now, for the diagram, think about it this way: could a single pathway like transport contain *both* adaptation and mitigation examples? What does that suggest about how the diagram is organized?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that adaptation is about responding to effects - I should briefly affirm this. Their follow-up confusion about pathways vs. sectors vs. actions is a real conceptual puzzle worth engaging with, but I shouldn't resolve it for them. I'll affirm their adaptation answer and then ask them a targeted question to help them see how the pathways and adaptation/mitigation might relate in the diagram.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah? that's why i'm confused tho, it feels like two diff systems stacked on each other and idk what the table is actually asking me to map.
exchanges.tutor: We've been circling this for a bit — I think the best move now is to just try. For the transport pathway in Jakarta, write me one concrete example of something the city is doing or facing. Don't worry yet about labeling it perfectly. What's one real thing you know about Jakarta and transport?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same structural confusion about the diagram. We've now had several exchanges on this meta-question of how the diagram is organized. Rather than continuing to discuss the structure abstractly, I should push the student to just attempt filling in one cell of the table for Jakarta — learning by doing will resolve this faster than continued discussion. This is a good moment to redirect to action.

exchanges.student: uhhh traffic, transit, flooding on roads, maybe MRT expansion? i'm kinda throwing stuff out bc idk what belongs where.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are all relevant. Now pick just one of those, say the MRT expansion. Is that something Jakarta is *experiencing* or something Jakarta is *doing*? And what does that tell you about where it might fit in the diagram?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced several concrete examples for Jakarta's transport pathway, which is real progress. Now I should help them start organizing rather than just listing — I'll ask them to pick one of those examples and think about whether it's something the city is doing (action) or something the city is experiencing (vulnerability/problem). This moves toward actually filling in the diagram without me doing it for them.

exchanges.student: it's doing, but that's where i get stuck bc then do i need one "doing" and one "experiencing" for every pathway or nah?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good instinct to check — the example diagram in the assignment should tell you whether a specific structure like that is required. But honestly, you now have enough to make a real attempt at the transport pathway entry for Jakarta. Why not write out what you'd actually put in the table for that pathway, and we can go from there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a very specific formatting question about the diagram structure. Rather than answering directly, I should redirect them to the example and assignment description, which should address this. Also, we've now had a productive enough conversation that the student should be able to attempt a written draft of at least the transport pathway. This is a good moment to push for a first written attempt.

deductions.reason: The tutor cycles repeatedly on the adaptation/mitigation distinction and the diagram structure question across multiple turns without consolidating or advancing. The student remains confused about how the 5 pathways relate to the diagram, and the tutor keeps asking similar probing questions (turns 5-8) without reframing, summarizing, or shifting strategy meaningfully until turn 8 when it finally pushes the student to just try.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice throughout, consistently refusing to give direct answers and instead guiding the student through questions. The student is never given submission-ready content, and key ideas remain student-generated. The one notable weakness is a pattern of repetitive cycling on the adaptation/mitigation and diagram-structure confusion across turns 5–8 without sufficient reframing or synthesis, which qualifies as a 2.1.A.a deduction. All other criteria are well-handled.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides final answers, never rewrites student work, and never reveals the conclusion before the student reasons toward it. Criterion 1.2 is intact: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., affirming 'responding' in turn 7, building on MRT in turns 9–10) and uses diagnostic questioning before responding. Criterion 1.3 is intact: the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., 'working through it yourself is exactly how it'll click,' directing the student to try one pathway first). For 2.1, the tutor cycles on the same confusion point — the relationship between adaptation/mitigation and the 5 pathways — across turns 5, 6, 7, and 8 without meaningfully reframing or consolidating, warranting a full 4-point deduction. Criteria 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 show no issues: the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses concise and interactive, and maintains a warm, non-evaluative tone throughout.
